SpotlightDescription: Darren Franich and Chancellor Agard kicked off Screen Drafts with the premiere episode, ranking the best third installments in film series. The show's first-ever veto occurred here when Franich rejected Agard's pick of The Dark Knight Rises; the first proposed "trades for unused vetoes" concept was also floated but scrapped after this episode.

Ryan Marker and Steve Berg drafted the best alien-visitor films with E.T. landing at #1. Two films were vetoed — Brother From Another Planet and Under the Skin — establishing the show's early veto patterns.

Angela Matano and Tom Cendejas competed over Hepburn's best films, with The Nun's Story claiming the top spot and Funny Face falling to a veto. The episode featured a clean sweep of Hepburn's most celebrated roles from Roman Holiday to Breakfast at Tiffany's.

Kyle Anderson and Billy Ray Brewton — billed as the first "Scream Drafts" episode — drafted top horror sequels with The Exorcist III taking the crown. Two films were vetoed and Brewton made his Screen Drafts debut here.

Piya Sinha-Roy and Darren Franich tackled millennial-era teen horror, with Sinha-Roy vetoing Franich's choice of It Follows and Raw ultimately claiming the top spot. Franich became the first drafter to appear in multiple Screen Drafts episodes.

Billy Ray Brewton and Heidi Honeycutt ranked King's best screen adaptations, placing Pet Sematary at #1. Both The Shining and Christine fell to vetoes, creating spirited debate about the most essential King adaptation.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker's first head-to-head draft became one of the most-discussed in series history, with 12 Monkeys topping the list. Ryan vetoed Clay's pick of Bill & Ted's Excellent Adventure, and the episode was later featured in The Marathon retrospective.

Maureen Lee Lenker and Vince Balzano compiled a vintage Christmas canon with It's a Wonderful Life taking #1. White Christmas was vetoed by Balzano after Lenker selected it — the episode was originally titled "Vintage Christmas."
Dave Schilling and Zed Cutsinger ranged across every sport with Eight Men Out earning the top spot. Rocky IV and Hoop Dreams were vetoed, pushing the list toward more understated sports cinema.

Billy Ray Brewton and Graham Skipper — Skipper's debut appearance — built a dark holiday canon with Gremlins at #1. Skipper became the third drafter to appear in three separate Screen Drafts in the same year.

Angela Matano and Alec Tibaldi drafted best films featuring notable nudity in service of character or story, with American Beauty earning the top spot. American Honey and Forgetting Sarah Marshall were both vetoed.

Amy Nicholson and Eva Anderson honored Christopher Lee's vast filmography with The Wicker Man landing at #1. Only one veto occurred — Star Wars Episode II — keeping the list focused on Lee's genre-defining roles.

The first Mega Draft featured Darren Franich, Clay Keller, and Ryan Marker picking the 17 best films of 2018, with First Reformed taking #1. Black Panther was initially vetoed before returning to the list, and BlacKkKlansman fell to another veto.

Graham Skipper and Sean Keller drafted the best twist-ending films, with The Wicker Man earning #1 and also becoming the first film inducted into Screen Drafts' Marquee of Fame. The Usual Suspects and Brazil were both vetoed.

James Ponsoldt and Darrin Navarro ranked the best films about filmmakers, placing Singin' in the Rain at the top. This episode introduced both the Commissioner Override and the Predictions Game — two cornerstone mechanics of the show.
Matt Perez-Mora and Holly Thompson locked horns over the Tarantino canon with Pulp Fiction taking #1. Kill Bill: Vol. 1 was initially vetoed before ultimately landing high on the list.

Billy Ray Brewton and Chris Thomas Devlin drafted the best '80s fantasy films with The NeverEnding Story at #1. Highlander and The Last Unicorn were vetoed, leaving a list anchored by Labyrinth and The Princess Bride.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker ranked amnesia films with Mulholland Drive at #1. This episode featured the first-ever Commissioner Override in series history, with Paris Texas removed from consideration.
Darren Franich and Jeff Jensen drafted Kubrick's filmography with 2001: A Space Odyssey at #1. Paths of Glory was vetoed and The Shining was initially vetoed before making the final list.
Dana Schwartz and Max Genecov ranked British romantic comedies with Bridget Jones's Diary at #1. Stardust was vetoed by Schwartz on her initial selection, leading to a revised pick.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker went head-to-head on the A24 catalogue with Moonlight at #1. No vetoes occurred, and Ryan won this early matchup — the episode was the first to record an official "Clay vs. Ryan" result.

A four-person Super Draft with Darren Franich, Clay Keller, Ryan Marker, and Chancellor Agard ranking all MCU films, Black Panther landing #1. Multiple vetoes including Spider-Man: Homecoming and Civil War made this one of the veto-heaviest episodes to that point.

Grant Moninger and Ryan Marker drafted the best dog films with Best in Show topping the list. The episode created the "Mooch Rule" — named after the obscure pick Mooch Goes to Hollywood — which limits overly esoteric selections.

Jesse Knight and Dane McDonald ranked the best Amy Adams performances with Enchanted at #1 (after Dane vetoed Jesse's initial Enchanted pick). Co-commissioner Britt Keller made a rare guest appearance.
Jenn Wilson and Drea Clark drafted the best films where actors play multiple roles, placing Dr. Strangelove at #1. Cloud Atlas' inclusion sparked debate; Coming to America was vetoed.

Elric Kane and Brian Saur ranked the best remakes with The Thing at #1 and earning a Marquee of Fame induction. Dirty Rotten Scoundrels and Sorcerer represented some of the list's most surprising inclusions.
Jim Branscome and Jonah Ray Rodrigues drafted the best punk-adjacent films with Repo Man at #1. Suburbia and Fat Kid Rules the World were vetoed, keeping the list centered on canonical punk cinema.

Thomas Grabinski and Eric Moore ranked Judd Apatow's best produced films with Superbad at #1. Get Him to the Greek was vetoed, and Grabinski's own Superbad selection was also initially vetoed before returning.

Clay Keller and Kate Hagen ranked the best erotic thrillers with Body Double topping the list. No vetoes occurred, and Clay won the matchup — the episode later became a reference point for the genre's critical rehabilitation.

Billy Ray Brewton and Darren Franich ranked films defined by their music with The Perks of Being a Wallflower at #1. The Big Chill was vetoed by Franich, setting up a debate about what "mixtape" really means.

Graham Skipper and Matt Mercer ranked creature feature films with Predator at #1. The Fly was removed via Commissioner Override, and The Thing earned its third appearance on a Screen Drafts list, achieving a rare "Hat Trick."
Oriana Nudo and Maureen Lee Lenker drafted Bogart's best films with We're No Angels at #1. Black Legion was vetoed in favor of The Maltese Falcon, resulting in an Nudo victory.

Bryan Cogman and Riley Stearns ranked Disney's hand-drawn animated films with Sleeping Beauty at #1. Mulan was vetoed and Pinocchio's initial selection was also rejected before being taken by Stearns.
Daniel Crooke and Mike Dougherty ranked the best queer romance films with Brokeback Mountain at #1. Bound earned a Marquee of Fame induction as the eleventh inductee.
Billy Ray Brewton and Matt Mercer ranked the best spoof films with Amazon Women on the Moon at #1. Team America: World Police was vetoed, shaping a list that leaned toward classic and cult spoofs.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker — with Billy Ray Brewton as Guest Commissioner — ranked the best films of 1999 with Notting Hill at #1. Ghost Dog: Way of the Samurai was vetoed; Notting Hill's #1 finish became one of the show's most surprising early results.

A four-drafter Super Draft with Rebekah McKendry, Elric Kane, Graham Skipper, and Billy Ray Brewton ranking every Nightmare on Elm Street and Friday the 13th film. Friday the 13th Part VI earned the top spot, and this episode introduced the first use of the veto override feature.
Clay Keller and Ryan Marker ranked their favorite films from the first half of 2019 with Booksmart at #1. Avengers: Endgame was vetoed by Ryan, one of the show's more memorable veto moments.

Oriana Nudo and Rance Collins ranked the best films featuring serious child characters with Now and Then at #1. E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial was inducted into the Marquee of Fame as the fourteenth film.

A four-person Mega Draft with Drea Clark, BenDavid Grabinski, John Freiler, and Marc Calderaro ranking 20 Nicolas Cage films with Mom and Dad in the top spot. Adaptation was vetoed and then entered the Marquee of Fame.
Ryan Marker and Patreon Members competed over the best films directed by actors with That Thing You Do! at #1. Night of the Hunter was initially vetoed before being selected by Marker.
Walter Hollmann and Ben Mekler ranked original American movie musicals with State Fair at #1. Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again was vetoed in the episode's most spirited disagreement.
Alonso Duralde and David Kittredge ranked Almodóvar's filmography with Law of Desire at #1. Bad Education was vetoed and then reselected, and Volver fell to a self-veto.
Nichol Lovett and Darrin Navarro ranked the best ensemble cast films with Underground at #1. It's a Mad Mad Mad Mad World was vetoed, setting up a list heavy on international cinema.

Kate Freund, Dave Schilling, Clay Keller, and Ricky Carmona ranked 20 '90s action films with Mission: Impossible at #1. El Mariachi and The Long Kiss Goodnight were vetoed; The Matrix and Face/Off entered the Marquee of Fame.

Joe Lynch and Drew McWeeny ranked the Carpenter filmography live with Prince of Darkness at #1. Big Trouble in Little China was initially vetoed before earning both the top spot and Marquee of Fame induction.

Kyle Anderson and Billy Ray Brewton ranked horror prequels with Exorcist: The Beginning at #1. A Commissioner Override removed Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom, citing it as more of a midquel than prequel.
Elric Kane and Graham Skipper drafted the best Halloween-set movies with Kenny & Company at #1. Night of the Demons was vetoed, and Halloween earned Marquee of Fame induction as the 20th inductee.
Clay Keller and Billy Ray Brewton ranked found footage films with Zero Day at #1. Faces of Death was vetoed, and The Blair Witch Project earned its Marquee of Fame induction as the 21st inductee.
Thomas Grabinski and Kevin Costello ranked the best heist films with Bottle Rocket at #1. National Treasure was removed via Commissioner Override and Sexy Beast was vetoed.

Bryan Cogman and Milla Bell-Hart ranked PSH's best performances with Magnolia at #1. Boogie Nights was vetoed and then reselected; The Master, The Talented Mr. Ripley, and Magnolia all entered the Marquee of Fame.

Morgan Peter Brown and Jeff Cannata ranked sword-and-sorcery films with The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring at #1. The Hobbit was removed via Commissioner Override, and The Princess Bride earned a Marquee of Fame induction.
Graham Skipper and Brea Grant ranked the best films about games with Tron at #1. Jumanji, Tommy, and The Wizard were all vetoed.

Darren Franich, Clay Keller, Alison Herman, and Piya Sinha-Roy ranked 20 films of the 2010s with Get Out at #1. Toni Erdmann was vetoed and Inherent Vice had a veto overridden by Franich — one of the decade draft's most contentious moments.
Alonso Duralde and Dave White ranked the best arthouse Christmas films with A Christmas Tale at #1. La Bûche was vetoed, keeping the list weighted toward international prestige titles.
Clay Keller and Ryan Marker ranked the best Kristen Stewart films on Christmas Day with Personal Shopper at #1. Both Certain Women and Personal Shopper entered the Marquee of Fame.

Clay Keller and Patreon Members ranked the best films of 2009 with Inglourious Basterds at #1. The episode aired on New Year's Eve and featured Patreon Members' collective voice as the opposing drafter.

Adam B. Vary, Devan Coggan, and Chancellor Agard ranked all Star Wars theatrical films with The Empire Strikes Back at #1. Rogue One was vetoed; The Empire Strikes Back earned Marquee of Fame induction; and this episode introduced rolling veto overrides.
Zoe Zelkind and Ryan Marker ranked the best Laura Dern performances with Citizen Ruth at #1. The list ranged from Blue Velvet to Little Women, celebrating Dern's extraordinary range.
Elric Kane and Rebekah McKendry ranked the best giallo films with Tenebre at #1. The Bird with the Crystal Plumage and A Lizard in a Woman's Skin were both vetoed.

Clay Keller, Marc Bernardin, Ryan Marker, and Darren Franich ranked all Star Trek films with Star Trek IV: The Voyage Home at #1. Multiple vetoes and a Commissioner Override on Star Trek: Generations led to an unusually chaotic draft.
Oriana Nudo, Billy Ray Brewton, Clay Keller, and Ryan Marker ranked 20 films of 2019 with Parasite at #1. Midsommar was vetoed twice by Ryan Marker, one of the show's most notable recurring vetoes.
Oriana Nudo and Rance Collins ranked films set during European vacations with Summertime at #1. An American Werewolf in London earned a Marquee of Fame induction.
Aaron LaPlante, Frank Dietz, and Kimmy Shields ranked 11 Universal Monster films with The Bride of Frankenstein at #1. Creature from the Black Lagoon was vetoed twice before finally making the list.
Drea Clark and Lucé Tomlin-Brenner ranked the best one-wild-night films with Attack the Block at #1. Halloween was vetoed; three films entered the Marquee of Fame.

Graham Skipper and Joe Begos ranked the best horror films of 1992 with Pet Sematary Two at #1. Candyman was vetoed before returning to the list, and Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me earned a Marquee of Fame induction.

Ryan Marker and Steve Berg ranked the best Cold War films with Dr. Strangelove at #1. 2010: The Year We Make Contact was vetoed; Dr. Strangelove achieved its third appearance on separate Screen Drafts lists.

Bryan Cogman and Billy Ray Brewton honored Max von Sydow's career with Pelle the Conqueror at #1. The Seventh Seal was vetoed; this episode was originally Patreon-exclusive but released publicly due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Clay Keller and Ryan Marker ranked post-apocalyptic films with Snowpiercer at #1. The Omega Man was vetoed; Mad Max: Fury Road, Snowpiercer, and Escape from New York entered the Marquee of Fame.
Dave Schilling and Halle Kiefer ranked the best Lorne Michaels-produced films with Wayne's World at #1. Kids in the Hall: Brain Candy was vetoed by Schilling before being reselected.
Graham Skipper and Brian Gillespie ranked Stuart Gordon's filmography with Re-Animator at #1. Edmond was vetoed; the episode celebrated Gordon's cult output from Re-Animator to Robot Jox.

Bryan Cogman and Maureen Lee Lenker ranked the best Warner Bros. films of the 1930s with The Adventures of Robin Hood at #1. The Petrified Forest was vetoed and The Adventures of Robin Hood was initially rejected before earning the top spot.

Billy Ray Brewton and Graham Skipper ranked the best adventure films for and about kids with Honey I Shrunk the Kids at #1. E.T. was selected for its third Screen Drafts appearance; The Sandlot was removed via Commissioner Override.
Darren Franich and Ryan Marker ranked Soderbergh's filmography with The Limey at #1. Contagion was vetoed; Ocean's Eleven and The Limey both entered the Marquee of Fame.
Bethy Squires and Jocey Coffman ranked the best contemporary camp films with Cats at #1. Moulin Rouge! was vetoed; Point Break entered the Marquee of Fame as the 60th inductee.

Joanna Robinson and Katey Rich ranked the best films about brief but transformative encounters with Brief Encounter at #1. Casablanca was vetoed by Robinson; Before Sunrise achieved its third appearance on separate Screen Drafts lists.
Maureen Lee Lenker and Oriana Nudo ranked the best mother-daughter films with Mildred Pierce at #1. Grey Gardens was vetoed; Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again earned its seventh Marquee of Fame Hat Trick.
Griffin Newman and J.D. Amato ranked the best Jim Henson productions with The Muppet Movie at #1. The Muppet Christmas Carol was vetoed; Labyrinth and The Muppet Movie entered the Marquee of Fame.
Brian Duffield and Drew McWeeny ranked Cronenberg's filmography with Dead Ringers at #1. The Dead Zone and The Fly both entered the Marquee of Fame.

Jared Moshe, Darren Franich, and Billy Ray Brewton ranked 13 classic Westerns with Stagecoach at #1. The Searchers was vetoed multiple times; this was the first installment in what became a beloved three-part western series.

Bryan Cogman and Ryan Marker ranked the best 1960s movie musicals with How to Succeed in Business Without Really Trying at #1. West Side Story and The Sound of Music were each vetoed before making the list.

Jordan Crucchiola and Drea Clark ranked the best mistaken-identity films with El Mariachi at #1. Two films — Much Ado About Nothing and The Talented Mr. Ripley — were removed via Commissioner Override, a rare double override.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker's 18-film Coen Brothers Super Draft placed Barton Fink at #1. The Big Lebowski was vetoed and then reselected — one of the show's most hotly contested vetoes; five films entered the Marquee of Fame.
Alison Willmore and Adam B. Vary ranked the best films traditionally released over July 4th weekend with Magic Mike XXL at #1. Independence Day and Terminator 2 were vetoed.
Dave Parker and Rebekah McKendry ranked the best heavy metal movies with Tenacious D in The Pick of Destiny at #1. The Dirt was vetoed as insufficiently metal in spirit.
William Bibbiani and Witney Seibold ranked the best nature-attack films with Shakma at #1. Creepshow was removed via Commissioner Override; Monkey Shines was vetoed.
Henry Zebrowski and Graham Skipper ranked the best sketch comedy anthology films with The Ten at #1. Kids in the Hall: Brain Candy was removed via Commissioner Override and Movie 43 was vetoed.

Demi Adejuyigbe and Ify Nwadiwe ranked the best con artist films with Now You See Me at #1. The episode was later retconned as "Modern Con Artists" after noting all selections were from 1999 or later.

Christian Holub and Devan Coggan ranked the best non-superhero comic book adaptations with Sin City at #1. The list featured a strong international presence with Akira, Persepolis, and Lady Snowblood.
Dana Schwartz and Phil Iscove ranked the best Winona Ryder films with Heathers at #1. Both Black Swan and Heathers were vetoed at various points before Heathers ultimately topped the list.

Darren Franich, Ryan Marker, and Billy Ray Brewton returned for 13 more Westerns with Ulzana's Raid at #1. The Good, the Bad and the Ugly entered the Marquee of Fame; multiple self-vetoes and override attempts shaped a contentious list.

Chelsea Stardust, Billy Ray Brewton, Graham Skipper, and Clarke Wolfe drafted 20 horror films of the 1970s with Invasion of the Body Snatchers at #1. This two-part Mega Draft aired over two consecutive episodes and featured Eraserhead, Suspiria, The Exorcist, and Jaws.
Matt Mercer and Dave Holmes ranked the best road trip films with The Sure Thing at #1. Paris, Texas earned the second spot; Y Tu Mamá También and The Muppet Movie rounded out a diverse list.

Jordan Crucchiola and Dave Schilling ranked the best Wesley Snipes films with White Men Can't Jump at #1. To Wong Foo Thanks for Everything was vetoed by Schilling; Disappearing Acts was removed via Commissioner Override under the Mooch Rule.

Maureen Lee Lenker, Bryan Cogman, and Graham Skipper ranked 11 Shakespeare film adaptations with Romeo and Juliet at #1. Romeo and Juliet was vetoed multiple times before landing at the top, making it one of the most contentious selections in the Shakespeare trilogy.
Clay Keller and Darren Franich ranked the best Florida-set crime films with Spring Breakers at #1. Body Heat and Wild Things were notable inclusions in a sun-drenched and genre-spanning list.

Rebekah McKendry and Elric Kane ranked the best Lucio Fulci films with The Beyond at #1. The Devil's Honey was vetoed; this episode also featured the first appearance of the Lord Bullingdon Rule during the trivia segment.

Kyle Anderson and Billy Ray Brewton ranked the best horror remakes with The Thing at #1. The Town that Dreaded Sundown was removed via Commissioner Override; The Thing (Brewton's version) was also vetoed.

Brian Collins, Scott Wampler, and Angie Han ranked every Alien and Predator franchise film with Alien at #1 for the show's 100th episode. Multiple vetoes and overrides made this a fittingly chaotic celebration; Predator entered the Marquee of Fame.

Jim Branscome and Alfonso Carrillo ranked the best films from the UK's Video Nasties prosecuted list with Night of the Demon at #1. Cannibal Holocaust was taken by Carrillo; Faces of Death was vetoed.

Guy Branum and Ryan Marker ranked the best films about the American presidency with Olympus Has Fallen taking the top spot. The episode aired days before the 2020 presidential election; Ryan appeared as drafter rather than commissioner.
Chris Feil and Joe Reid ranked the best drag-centric films with Bad Education at #1. Connie and Carla and To Wong Foo were each vetoed; three films entered the Marquee of Fame.
Frank Dietz and Frank H. Woodward ranked the best 1950s science fiction films with The Beast From 20,000 Fathoms at #1. Forbidden Planet was vetoed by Dietz; Godzilla entered the Marquee of Fame.

Angelique Jackson, Adam B. Vary, Griffin Newman, and Dane McDonald ranked all 22 Pixar films with Cars 2 taking the last spot and Ratatouille at #1. Five vetoes were exercised including Cars 3 and Up; Toy Story 3 entered the Marquee of Fame.
Halle Kiefer and Alison Leiby ranked the best films featuring on-screen friendships with Fools Rush In at #1. Scream earned Marquee of Fame induction as the 96th inductee.
Drew McWeeny and Alan Sepinwall ranked the best films adapted from TV shows with Miami Vice at #1. Star Trek Beyond earned Marquee of Fame status as the 97th inductee.

Billy Ray Brewton, Drea Clark, and Darren Franich completed the three-part Western series with True History of the Kelly Gang at #1. Slow West was almost vetoed out by Franich before a Commissioner Override by Brewton saved it, and both Slow West and The Hateful Eight entered the Marquee of Fame.

Bryan Cogman and Pat Driscoll ranked every major A Christmas Carol adaptation with An American Christmas Carol at #1. This was the 100th film inducted into the Marquee of Fame — Scrooge (1951) — a milestone celebrated on the episode.

Clay Keller, Ryan Marker, and Thomas Grabinski drafted all 16 Assayas features in a Christmas Day Super Draft with Wasp Network at #1. Five vetoes and a Commissioner Override shaped a deep-cut auteur exercise; Clouds of Sils Maria and Summer Hours entered the Marquee of Fame.

Bryan Cogman, Graham Skipper, and Joanna Robinson returned for 11 more Shakespeare adaptations (1988-2020) with Much Ado About Nothing at #1. Twelfth Night, Romeo + Juliet, and Titus were all vetoed; the mini-mega format and new era of Shakespeare cinema distinguished it from Part I.

Morgan Peter Brown and BenDavid Grabinski ranked the Tony Scott filmography with Crimson Tide at #1. True Romance was vetoed by Grabinski and Man on Fire was self-vetoed by Brown before making the list.

David Chen and Dan Trachtenberg ranked John Woo's best films with Hard Boiled at #1. Hard Target was vetoed by Chen; the episode featured discussion of Woo's Hong Kong output vs. his Hollywood period.
Rebekah McKendry and David Ian McKendry ranked the best aquatic horror films with Deep Rising at #1. Shark attack films were explicitly excluded from consideration; Triangle was vetoed.
Kay Hanley, Dave Holmes, and Matt Mercer ranked the best films about fictional musicians with Almost Famous at #1. Sing Street and Green Room were vetoed; six films entered the Marquee of Fame.

Patrick Bromley and Elric Kane ranked the best Cannon Films productions with Cobra at #1. Cyborg and Lifeforce were both vetoed in a draft that celebrated Cannon's wild output from Death Wish 3 to Runaway Train.

In a Valentine's Day special, married couples Chelsea Stardust & Sean Keller vs. Andrew Merrill & Beth Crudele drafted horror films about relationships with Rosemary's Baby at #1. The first team-format draft; Christine and The Shining entered the Marquee of Fame.
Wynter Mitchell and Guy Branum ranked the best Hugh Grant films with Four Weddings and a Funeral at #1. No vetoes occurred — a clean sweep from About a Boy to Paddington 2.

Ricky Carmona and Dave Schilling ranked the best Eddie Murphy films with Coming to America at #1. Eddie Murphy: Delirious was removed via Commissioner Override under the Mooch Rule; Coming to America was initially vetoed before landing at #1.

Drew McWeeny and Bryan Cogman ranked Disney live-action adventure films from 1950-1980 with Swiss Family Robinson at #1. Kidnapped and 20,000 Leagues Under the Sea were both vetoed; six films entered the Marquee of Fame.

Graham Skipper and Miguel Rodriguez ranked the best Godzilla and King Kong films with Godzilla: Final Wars at #1. Both commissioners achieved strong prediction accuracy in this monster-movie showdown.

Patrick Cotnoir and Connor Ratliff ranked Lucas's directorial and expanded filmography with THX 1138 at #1. Star Wars Detours was removed via Commissioner Override; the episode featured a nuanced defense of the prequel trilogy.
Phil Iscove and Kenny Neibart ranked the best Bruckheimer-produced films with Black Hawk Down at #1. Con Air was vetoed by Iscove in one of the episode's memorable disputes.

Clay Keller, Darren Franich, and Drea Clark ranked 11 Minnesota-set films with Beautiful Girls at #1. God's Country was removed via Commissioner Override; Drop Dead Gorgeous and Jennifer's Body landed on the list in a state-specific deep dive.

Bryan Cogman and Milla Bell-Hart ranked the best private eye films with Harper at #1. Night Moves was vetoed by Bell-Hart; The Maltese Falcon was self-vetoed by Cogman before landing in the top seven.

Ryan Marker and Lucé Tomlin-Brenner drafted all of Reichardt's features with Old Joy at #1. Meek's Cutoff and First Cow were each vetoed; Ryan participated as drafter so no commissioner predictions were made.

Rachel Walker and Anam Syed ranked the best female friendship films with The Spy Who Dumped Me at #1. Both Beaches and Romy and Michele's High School Reunion were vetoed before eventually appearing elsewhere on the list.

Ryan Marker, Angelique Jackson, Billy Ray Brewton, and Drea Clark ranked 20 films of 2020 with Miss Juneteenth at #1. Promising Young Woman was vetoed twice — by both Brewton and Clark — in one of the most striking repeat vetoes in Mega Draft history.

Jordan Crucchiola and Sam Wineman ranked the best slasher films of the 2000s with Sorority Row at #1. Crucchiola dominated with four of the seven selections, and no vetoes were needed in this deep dive into Y2K-era slashers.
Chris Feil and Joe Reid ranked the best films in which an actress won the Best Actress Oscar in the 21st century, with Monster (2003) at #1. Both commissioners achieved strong prediction accuracy.
Mark Harris and Adam B. Vary ranked the best films about the television industry with Broadcast News at #1. No vetoes or overrides occurred in this clean, press-friendly draft.

Bryan Cogman, Joanna Robinson, Graham Skipper, and Maureen Lee Lenker completed the Shakespeare trilogy with Shakespeare in Love at #1. West Side Story was vetoed twice before Robinson secured it; Throne of Blood's veto was overridden by Cogman.
Robert Butler III and Andres Cabrera ranked the best directorial debuts of the 2010s with Get Out at #1. Deadpool was vetoed; this episode also introduced what became known as the Cassavetes Rule.

Kate Hagen and Jen Johans ranked the best James Spader performances with Crash (1996) at #1. The episode began Predictions Game Season 4; Secretary and Sex, Lies, and Videotape rounded out a list heavy on Spader's more transgressive work.
Darren Franich, Ryan Marker, and Clay Keller ranked 13 X-Men franchise films with Logan at #1. Logan's veto was overridden by Ryan Marker; X-Men: Apocalypse earned a controversial high placement.

Kristy Puchko and Angie Han ranked the best films about internet culture with Unfriended at #1. Unfriended was initially self-vetoed by Han before landing at #1; this episode also clarified the Mooch Rule to allow major post-COVID streaming titles.

Drew McWeeny and Alan Sepinwall ranked the best TV-based film continuations with The Muppet Movie at #1. The Muppet Movie was vetoed by McWeeny before Sepinwall took it; the episode served as a companion to their TV Adaptations draft.

Graham Skipper and Rebekah McKendry ranked all Hellraiser films in a live episode with Hellraiser at #1. Hellraiser: Bloodline was vetoed by Skipper; Clay did not make commissioner predictions due to the live format.
Maureen Lee Lenker and Oriana Nudo ranked the best Natalie Wood films with West Side Story at #1. West Side Story was vetoed twice — twice by Lenker and once by Nudo — before finally landing at #1.

Dave Gonzales and Neil Miller ranked the best "legacy sequel" films that reunite decades-later casts with Mad Max: Fury Road at #1. Dawn of the Dead was vetoed; Blade Runner 2049 and Incredibles 2 rounded out the top picks.

Kevin Costello and Thomas Grabinski ranked the best video game adaptations with Resident Evil: Extinction at #1. Final Fantasy: The Spirits Within and Detective Pikachu were vetoed; Street Fighter earned a surprise high placement.

Drea Clark and Bryan Cogman ranked the best films of 2007 with There Will Be Blood at #1. Zodiac was initially vetoed by Cogman before landing at #4; No Country for Old Men and 4 Months, 3 Weeks and 2 Days also made the list.
Dylan Guerra and Clarke Wolfe ranked Craven's filmography live with Scream at #1. Scream 3 was vetoed; the live format meant no commissioner predictions were made.

Ryan Marker and Marya Gates ranked the best Bob Dylan documentary films with Rolling Thunder Revue at #1. Hearts of Fire and Rolling Thunder Revue were both vetoed; the episode focused on Dylan's film appearances and concert documents.
Billy Ray Brewton and Harmony Colangelo ranked all Tremors franchise films with Tremors at #1. Multiple Tremors 5 and Shrieker Island selections were vetoed in this affectionate franchise deep-dive.

Wynter Mitchell, Jordan Crucchiola, Marc Bernardin, and Drew McWeeny ranked 20 films featuring Bruce Willis, Arnold Schwarzenegger, and Sylvester Stallone with Nighthawks at #1. Last Action Hero, True Lies, and Predator were all vetoed; Batman & Robin and Death Becomes Her earned unexpected inclusions.
Maureen Lee Lenker and Joanna Robinson ranked the best Jane Austen adaptations with Sense and Sensibility at #1. Emma. and Persuasion were vetoed; Clueless and Love & Friendship both made the list.

Alexei Toliopoulos and Blake Howard ranked the best Australian films of the 21st century with Goldstone at #1. The episode featured a rich survey of recent Aussie cinema from Animal Kingdom to Samson and Delilah.

Andrew Roebuck and Steven Ray Morris ranked the best dinosaur films with Jurassic Park at #1. The Tree of Life was vetoed for its brief dinosaur cameo; Super Mario Bros. earned a spirited defense for its dinosaur-adjacent world.
Jim Branscome and Breanna Whipple ranked the best Canadian exploitation films with FUBAR at #1. The episode dove deep into CanCon cult cinema from Fast Company to Hobo with a Shotgun.
Rebekah McKendry and Elric Kane ranked the best body horror films with Tetsuo: The Iron Man at #1. Body Parts was vetoed twice by McKendry; the list ranged from The Fly to Society.

Amanda Smith, Billy Ray Brewton, and Kenny Neibart ranked 11 baseball films with Everybody Wants Some!! at #1. The Sandlot and Bull Durham were vetoed; A League of Their Own earned a surprise veto before being rescued.
Kyle Anderson and Billy Ray Brewton ranked the best Dimension Films releases with Halloween H20 at #1. Grindhouse was vetoed; the list celebrated Dimension's genre output from Scream to The Mist.
Graham Skipper and Halle Kiefer ranked the best mad-scientist films with Event Horizon at #1. Young Frankenstein appeared midlist; The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari earned its place as the genre's ur-text.

Clarke Wolfe, Darren Franich, and Clark Collis ranked 13 zombie films with Night of the Comet at #1. Night of the Living Dead was self-vetoed before a Franich override saved it; ParaNorman and I Walked with a Zombie were unexpected highlights.

Dave Schilling, Phil Nobile Jr./Drew McWeeny, Piya Sinha-Roy, and Bryan Cogman ranked all 25 Bond films across two parts. Casino Royale topped Part II; the format allowed the bottom 13 to be ranked in Part I with the top 13 in Part II, featuring Drew McWeeny replacing Phil Nobile Jr.

Andrew Furtado and Scott Reynolds ranked the best serial killer films with No Way to Treat a Lady at #1. Man Bites Dog was self-vetoed; I Saw the Devil was vetoed, creating a list centered on procedural and psychological depth.

Chris Feil and Joe Reid ranked the best Oscar-winning queer films with The Times of Harvey Milk at #1. The Crying Game and All About My Mother were vetoed in this live episode with no commissioner predictions.

Adam B. Vary and Alicia Lutes ranked films historically released over Thanksgiving weekend with Creed II at #1. Toy Story 2, Frozen, and Knives Out all made the list alongside holiday perennial Mrs. Doubtfire.
Sarah Sterling and Ash Crossan ranked the best DCOMs with Cadet Kelly at #1. Smart House was vetoed by Sterling; the episode was a love letter to '90s and early 2000s Disney Channel nostalgia.

Kenny Neibart and Phil Iscove ranked the best films that won the Razzie for Worst Picture with Mommie Dearest at #1. Howard the Duck was vetoed initially before landing on the list; Color of Night was vetoed.

Libby Hill and Emily St. James ranked the best films set at or around Christmas but not explicitly Christmas movies, with Rocky IV at #1. It's a Wonderful Life was vetoed by Hill; the list included Carol, Eyes Wide Shut, and In Bruges.

Chris Scleicher and Guy Branum ranked the best films about the British royal family with Elizabeth at #1. Anne of the Thousand Days and The Queen were both vetoed; the episode aired in the wake of The Crown's popularity.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker — with Billy Ray Brewton as commissioner — ranked the best Rebecca Hall performances on Christmas Eve with The Gift at #1. Professor Marston was vetoed by Clay; this was part of the show's Kristmas Eve tradition.
Oriana Nudo and Ryan Marker ranked the Wilder filmography with Love in the Afternoon at #1. Some Like It Hot was controversially vetoed by Ryan — one of the show's most debated vetoes of 2022.

Harmony Colangelo and B.J. Colangelo ranked the best adult animated films with Coonskin at #1. Princess Mononoke, Persepolis, and Perfect Blue all made the list; the episode was a showcase for animated films that push against family expectations.

Billy Ray Brewton, Clarke Wolfe, and Graham Skipper ranked 11 alien invasion films live at Fantastic Fest 2021 with Evolution at #1. Mac and Me was removed via Commissioner Override; Cloverfield's veto was overridden by Brewton.

Jarrod Murray and Evan Dickson ranked the best films about screenwriters with The TV Set at #1. Barton Fink was self-vetoed before returning to the list; the episode was co-commissioned by Phil Iscove.
Alan Sepinwall and Linda Holmes ranked the best NASA-related films with Hidden Figures at #1. The Right Stuff was vetoed by Holmes; the list ranged from SpaceCamp to Gravity.
Wynter Mitchell and Guy Branum ranked the best Whoopi Goldberg films with Made in America at #1. Sister Act was vetoed by Branum; Boys on the Side and The Long Walk Home were discarded.

Carla Renata and Angelique Jackson ranked the best Black love stories on film with A Warm December at #1. Jumping the Broom and Waiting to Exhale were both vetoed; the list ranged from If Beale Street Could Talk to How Stella Got Her Groove Back.

Chancellor Agard and Derek Lawrence ranked the best Will Smith films with Independence Day at #1. Bad Boys II and Six Degrees of Separation were both vetoed; Ali earned a high placement despite limited box office awareness.

Larry Zerner and Marc Calderaro ranked the best courtroom dramas with Primal Fear at #1. My Cousin Vinny was vetoed before returning; the list included classics from Anatomy of a Murder to The Devil's Advocate.

Marc Bernardin, Clarke Wolfe, Devan Coggan, and Ryan Marker ranked every Batman and Superman film with Zack Snyder's Justice League at #1. Multiple veto overrides occurred including one saving Batman Returns; Ryan participated as a drafter making this one of the format's most chaotic.
David Ian McKendry and Rebekah McKendry ranked the best crocodilian horror films with Dark Age at #1. Croc was vetoed; the episode featured the McKendrys' encyclopedic knowledge of the subgenre.

Alexei Toliopoulos and Cameron James ranked the best Happy Madison films with Joe Dirt at #1. Sandy Wexler was vetoed; the episode leaned into the show's contrarian spirit by championing underrated Sandler output.

Oriana Nudo, Billy Ray Brewton, Clay Keller, and Drea Clark ranked 20 films of 2021 with Shiva Baby at #1. Last Night in Soho, Malignant, and C'mon C'mon were all vetoed; The Worst Person in the World was vetoed at #16 and then rescued by a commissioner override.

Anna Bogutskaya and Jordan Crucchiola ranked the best female-led action films of the 21st century with Chocolate at #1. The list featured Atomic Blonde, Kill Bill: Vol. 1, and Birds of Prey — a vibrant survey of women leading action cinema.

Simon Abrams and Drea Clark ranked the Spheeris filmography with The Little Rascals at #1. Suburbia was vetoed twice; the episode was recorded for Skyline Indie Film Fest but released on the main feed.

Frank Dietz and Frank H. Woodward ranked the best Ray Harryhausen films with Mighty Joe Young at #1. 20 Million Miles to Earth was vetoed before returning; the two Franks' expertise made this a deep dive into stop-motion history.

Scott Beck, Bryan Woods, Jonathan Baker, and Josh Baker ranked 14 Shyamalan films with Unbreakable at #1. Signs was vetoed by Josh Baker before being rescued by Bryan Woods via override; the four drafters were the Haunt and Stopmotion directors.

Darren Franich and Bryan Cogman ranked Welles's filmography with Citizen Kane at #1. Othello was vetoed; the episode was billed as the finale of "Auteur April" and the first head-to-head between these two frequent guests.
Graham Skipper and Jenelle Riley ranked the best space horror films with Alien at #1. Aliens was vetoed by Skipper; Clarke Wolfe served as guest co-commissioner.

Alan Sepinwall and Daniel Fienberg ranked the best sports films of the 1970s with Breaking Away at #1. Breaking Away was vetoed by Fienberg before returning; the episode kicked off what became a multi-part sports series with these two drafters.
William Bibbiani and Liz Shannon Miller ranked the best dance films with Dirty Dancing at #1. Center Stage was vetoed; Bibbiani swept the commissioner predictions 3-0.

Katey Rich, Wynter Mitchell, Kristy Puchko, and Clay Keller ranked 13 films of 1997 with Titanic at #1. Chasing Amy was vetoed; Boogie Nights had a veto overridden by Mitchell — one of the more contentious moments in the mini-Mega format.
Phil Iscove and Kenny Neibart ranked the best hotel and motel movies with The Shining at #1. No vetoes occurred; the list ranged from Barton Fink to Dirty Pretty Things.

Jason Shawhan and Billy Ray Brewton ranked the Waters filmography with Polyester at #1. Pink Flamingos was vetoed by Shawhan; Polyester was vetoed by Brewton before claiming the top spot in an unexpected #1 choice.
Mark Harris and Adam B. Vary ranked the best directorial debuts of the 1990s with Hard Eight at #1. Reservoir Dogs was vetoed; Being John Malkovich fell to another veto.

Chris Feil, Gavin Mevius, Luis Rendon, and Joe Reid ranked the most interesting films to receive an F CinemaScore with Solaris at #1. Mother! was vetoed twice and then overridden; Silent House was also vetoed.
Jordan Crucchiola and Roxana Hadadi ranked the best Ryan Gosling films with First Man at #1. The Notebook was vetoed; the list favored Gosling's more adventurous and dramatic work.

Brian Duffield and Thomas Grabinski ranked the best Soderbergh films from his post-retirement "comeback" period with Kimi at #1. High Flying Bird was vetoed; Kimi was vetoed twice before landing at #1. Duffield won 3-1.

Kate Hagen and Drew McWeeny ranked films that received an X, NC-17, or Unrated rating with The Devils at #1. Irréversible was vetoed; the list featured The Cook the Thief His Wife and Her Lover, Y Tu Mamá También, and Pink Flamingos.

Bryan Cogman and John Bradley ranked the best Beatles films with A Hard Day's Night at #1. A Hard Day's Night was initially vetoed before landing at #1; John Bradley (of Game of Thrones) made his Screen Drafts debut.

Alonso Duralde, Dory Benford, Rachel Wagner, and Daniel Thompson ranked 12 Hallmark Christmas films with A Very Merry Mix-Up at #1. Multiple vetoes occurred and A Very Merry Mix-Up's veto was overridden — a rare example of a Hallmark film generating genuine controversy.

Joanna Robinson and Bryan Cogman ranked the best Agatha Christie adaptations with Death on the Nile (1978) at #1. The episode featured Christie's full range from classic BBC productions to contemporary films; Cogman won 3-1.
Marc Bernardin, Jacqueline Coley, and Kevin Avery ranked 11 Denzel Washington films with Fences at #1. Man on Fire was vetoed twice; Inside Man fell to another veto.
Maureen Lee Lenker and Oriana Nudo ranked the best films that were their director's final work with Imitation of Life at #1. Lenker won 3-0; the list included Eyes Wide Shut and Three Colours: Red.
Scott Reynolds and Blake Masters ranked the Peckinpah filmography with The Wild Bunch at #1. The Ballad of Cable Hogue was vetoed; Masters won 3-0.

Lucé Tomlin-Brenner and Ryan Marker ranked the best Varda films with One Sings the Other Doesn't at #1. One Sings the Other Doesn't was vetoed by Marker before he reselected it; the episode honored Varda's entire career from Cléo from 5 to 7 to her final works.

Graham Skipper, Beth Accomando, and Drew & Toshi McWeeny ranked 13 Godzilla films with Godzilla (Beth Accomando's pick) at #1. Godzilla vs. Destoroyah and Shin Godzilla were both vetoed; Drew McWeeny and his son Toshi co-drafted as a team.

Jason Sheridan and Eva Anderson ranked the best vintage con artist films — the "sequel" to the earlier Con Artist episode — with F for Fake at #1. The Grifters, The Sting, and Paper Moon all made the list.

Phil Iscove and Dana Schwartz ranked the best Kirsten Dunst films with Marie Antoinette at #1. A withdrawn veto by Schwartz created a notable moment; The Virgin Suicides, Eternal Sunshine, and Melancholia rounded out the list.

Kyle Anderson and Billy Ray Brewton ranked the best A24 horror films with Green Room at #1. Hereditary and Green Room were both vetoed in the opening rounds; Under the Skin earned a top-five placement.

Rebekah McKendry, David Ian McKendry, and Morgan Peter Brown ranked 11 horror comedies with Evil Dead II at #1. One Cut of the Dead had its veto overridden; An American Werewolf in London and Shaun of the Dead earned high placements.

Patrick Bromley and Elric Kane ranked Hooper's filmography with The Texas Chain Saw Massacre at #1. Toolbox Murders and The Mangler were vetoed; Lifeforce and Poltergeist represented Hooper's studio-era work.

Wynter Mitchell, Patrick Hamilton, Renée Bever, and April Wolfe ranked 13 high school horror films on Halloween with Halloween at #1. Ryan Marker drafted on behalf of the absent April Wolfe — a notable moment of proxy drafting.
Darren Franich and Darrin Navarro ranked the Kurosawa filmography with Ran at #1. High and Low was vetoed twice; Dodes'ka-den and I Live in Fear earned inclusion as underappreciated Kurosawa works.
Billy Ray Brewton and Ryan Marker ranked the best teacher films with School of Rock at #1. October Sky was removed via Commissioner Override; Another Round was vetoed.

Oriana Nudo, Walter Hollmann, and Maureen Lee Lenker ranked 11 Paul Newman films with Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid at #1. The Life and Times of Judge Roy Bean, Slap Shot, and Harry & Son were all vetoed.

Louis Peitzman, Devan Coggan, Chancellor Agard, and Adam B. Vary ranked every MCU Phase 4 film and TV series — the first Screen Drafts episode to include TV seasons alongside films. The "Feige Dispensation" was introduced; Loki topped the list.

Joe Reid, Katey Rich, and Chris Feil ranked films directed by filmmakers after winning the Best Picture Oscar with Apocalypse Now at #1. Broadcast News was vetoed twice; the list rewarded directors who followed their Oscar success with ambitious work.
Bryan Cogman and Helen Shang ranked the best Arthurian legend films with Excalibur at #1. The Green Knight was vetoed by Shang; Cogman self-vetoed Excalibur before ultimately selecting it #1.
Graham Skipper and Eric Pennycoff ranked the best Christmas horror films with Deadly Games at #1. Gremlins was vetoed by Pennycoff; the list featured Rare Exports and Black Christmas.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker ranked the best Colin Farrell films on Christmas Eve in the Kristmas Eve tradition with The New World at #1. The Beguiled, The New World, and The Banshees of Inisherin were all vetoed; the nine-film mini-mega format was a departure from the standard seven.

A three-part Super Draft across January 2023 with Drea Clark, Darren Franich, Drew McWeeny, Roxana Hadadi, Billy Ray Brewton, Angelique Jackson, Wynter Mitchell, Adam B. Vary, and Lucé Tomlin-Brenner ranking Spielberg's full filmography with Jaws at #1. A Commissioner Override removed Jaws in Part II before it was ultimately rescued; multiple veto overrides shaped the final list.

Three teams — Amanda Smith & Jordan Crucchiola, Phil Iscove & Kenny Neibart, and Clay Keller & Ryan Marker — ranked Spielberg-produced films across two parts with Back to the Future at #1. Twister's veto was overridden; the team format made this a structurally distinctive Mega Draft.

Mikelle Street and Tre'vell Anderson ranked the best Black queer films with Still Black: A Portrait of Black Transmen at #1. Recorded live for the Seattle Queer Film Festival, this episode featured no canonical predictions and a list spanning Paris Is Burning to Moonlight.

Jordan Crucchiola and Sam Wineman ranked the best obsession thriller films with Lisa at #1. The Cable Guy and Black Swan were both vetoed; the list showcased the erotic and psychological ends of the subgenre.

B.J. Colangelo and Louis Peitzman ranked all 8 Chucky franchise films with Child's Play 2 at #1. Seed of Chucky was vetoed by Peitzman before returning; both drafters brought deep franchise knowledge to the debate.

Mitchell Beaupre and Slim ranked the films that won the Independent Spirit Award for Best First Feature with Dirty Dancing at #1. The list ranged from You Can Count on Me to Sound of Metal — a survey of American indie debuts.

Jacqueline Coley, Drea Clark, Ryan Marker, and Emily St. James ranked 20 films of 2022 with RRR at #1. All Quiet on the Western Front, Babylon, and Tár were all vetoed; Everything Everywhere All at Once made the list despite its heavy awards presence.

Clarke Wolfe and Anthony DiBlasi ranked the best cult-focused films with Hereditary at #1. Mandy was vetoed by Wolfe; the list featured Rosemary's Baby, The Wicker Man, and Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood.
Chris Amick and Ben Mekler ranked the best train movies with The Train at #1. The Polar Express was vetoed; Emperor of the North and The General represented the list's classical end.

Bryan Cogman and Darrin Navarro ranked the best 1970s conspiracy thrillers with The Conversation at #1. Invasion of the Body Snatchers was vetoed; All the President's Men and The Parallax View captured the paranoid spirit of the decade.

Brea Grant, Clarke Wolfe, and Billy Ray Brewton ranked 9 robot films live at an event with Blade Runner at #1. Jason X was removed via Commissioner Override; multiple vetoes and overrides made this an eventful live draft.
Dana Schwartz and Phil Iscove ranked the best Anne Hathaway films with The Devil Wears Prada at #1. The Intern was vetoed by Iscove; the list included Rachel Getting Married and Les Misérables.

Billy Ray Brewton and Marya Gates ranked the best music documentaries with The Devil and Daniel Johnston at #1. The Devil and Daniel Johnston was self-vetoed by Brewton before Gates claimed it; Summer of Soul earned a high placement.

Joe Reid, Liz Hannah, and Kenny Neibart ranked the best John Grisham adaptations with The Pelican Brief at #1. A Time to Kill was vetoed; Christmas with the Kranks earned a spirited defense as a Grisham oddity.
Maria Lewis and Drea Clark ranked the best films of 2012 with The Dark Knight Rises at #1. Byzantium was removed via Commissioner Override; The Cabin in the Woods was vetoed.
Eric Plese and Ryan Estrada ranked the Bong filmography with Memories of Murder at #1. The Host was vetoed by Estrada — the veto was delivered by an actor from the film in a memorable bit.

Darren Franich, Jen Yamato, and Piya Sinha-Roy ranked 13 Dwayne Johnson and Vin Diesel films with Fast Five at #1. Multiple vetoes including The Scorpion King and Jungle Cruise; Moana, The Iron Giant, and Furious 7 all made the list.

Louis Peitzman and Oriana Nudo ranked the best jukebox musicals with Singin' in the Rain at #1. Across the Universe and Singin' in the Rain were each vetoed; Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again earned a polarizing top-five finish.

Drea Clark and Guy Branum ranked the best Merchant Ivory productions with A Room with a View at #1. Branum vetoed A Room with a View on his first selection before ultimately choosing it #1 — a notable veto rescission.
Adam B. Vary and Jordan Crucchiola ranked the best queer-coded or queer-themed studio films with Victor/Victoria at #1. Victor/Victoria was vetoed by Crucchiola before earning the top spot.

Joanna Robinson, Mallory Rubin, and Bryan Cogman ranked 13 Harrison Ford films with Raiders of the Lost Ark at #1. Air Force One was vetoed after a Trump reference was noted; Regarding Henry and Frantic earned their first-ever Screen Drafts consideration.

Marc Bernardin and Ryan Marker ranked all 9 Rocky and Creed films with Creed at #1. Rocky III and Rocky IV were each vetoed; the episode was a love letter to the franchise's resilience over four decades.
Bryan Cogman, Oriana Nudo, and Maureen Lee Lenker ranked 11 screwball comedies with It Happened One Night at #1. Bringing Up Baby, Arsenic and Old Lace, and Christmas in Connecticut were all vetoed.

Kenny Neibart and Liz Hannah ranked the best films about labor unions with Newsies at #1. Silkwood was vetoed; the episode aired during Hollywood's 2023 strikes, giving it an especially charged context.
Andy Levy and Mike Ryan ranked the best fifth installments in film series with Last Action Hero at #1. The episode coined the term "Fivequel" as a concept category.

Darren Franich and Dave Schilling ranked the best '90s superhero films with Judge Dredd at #1. Darkman was vetoed by Franich; The Shadow and The Rocketeer made the list alongside the more expected Blade and Batman Returns.
Ian Karmel and Sean Jordan ranked the best George Clooney films with The Perfect Storm at #1. From Dusk Till Dawn was vetoed; O Brother Where Art Thou? and Three Kings earned high placements.

Clarke Wolfe and Eva Anderson ranked the best films directed by screenwriters as their first film with Ex Machina at #1. No vetoes or overrides occurred; the list spanned The Maltese Falcon to Promising Young Woman.

Libby Hill and Emily St. James ranked the best country music films with O Brother Where Art Thou? at #1. The Broken Circle Breakdown was vetoed by St. James; Nashville and Coal Miner's Daughter also made the list.

Kyle Turner and Juan Barquin ranked the best queer crime films with Can You Ever Forgive Me? at #1. Kajillionaire was vetoed; Scorpio Rising was removed via Commissioner Override for being a short film.

Billy Ray Brewton, Drea Clark, and Drew McWeeny ranked 11 Vietnam War films with The Boys in Company C at #1. The Deer Hunter was vetoed by Brewton; multiple vetoes and a Commissioner Override shaped this five-hour marathon episode.

Devan Coggan and Piya Sinha-Roy ranked the best Greta Gerwig films with Hannah Takes the Stairs at #1. No vetoes or overrides occurred; Barbie's inclusion as a top-five pick reflected the film's cultural moment.

Clay Keller, Thomas Grabinski, Karen Tongson, and Helen Shang ranked 13 films of 1998 with Velvet Goldmine at #1 in Shang's pick. Rounders and Funny Games were both vetoed; Deep Impact and Mulan earned surprise inclusions.
Kyle Anderson and Billy Ray Brewton ranked the best Blumhouse productions with The Town That Dreaded Sundown at #1. Get Out was vetoed by Anderson; Halloween Kills was vetoed by Brewton.
Wynter Mitchell and Morgan Peter Brown ranked the best home invasion films with Knock Knock at #1. Home Alone, Mother!, and Funny Games (2007) were all vetoed; Morgan Peter Brown won 3-0.

Matt Singer and Jordan Hoffman ranked films that were negatively reviewed by Siskel and Ebert (two thumbs down) with Last Action Hero at #1. Army of Darkness was vetoed by Hoffman; Matt Singer won 3-0.
Megan Amram, Walter Hollmann, and Ben Mekler ranked all 10 Saw films with Spiral at #1. Saw X was vetoed twice and Saw was vetoed; Megan Amram won the first-ever three-way Saw Super Draft.

Rebekah McKendry, Elric Kane, Graham Skipper, and Billy Ray Brewton ranked all 13 Halloween films with Halloween Ends at #1. A veto override by Skipper on Halloween Kills shaped the final rankings; Billy Ray Brewton came first.
Joe Reid and Adam B. Vary ranked 10 Don Bluth animated films with A Troll in Central Park at #1. An American Tail was vetoed twice by Reid; Adam B. Vary won 3-0.

B.J. Colangelo, Louis Peitzman, Rebekah McKendry, and Dane McDonald ranked all 26+ Disney animated feature films across two parts with Snow White at #14 and Sleeping Beauty at #1. Multiple vetoes and overrides including a Hunchback of Notre Dame override; Dane McDonald came first.

William Bibbiani and Harmony Colangelo ranked 10 Ralph Bakshi films with Cool World at #1. The Lord of the Rings and Fritz the Cat rounded out a list that surveyed Bakshi's provocative animated output; Bibbiani won 3-1.
Oriana Nudo and Maureen Lee Lenker ranked the best Judy Garland films with Summer Stock at #1. Lenker won 3-1; the list ranged from The Wizard of Oz to A Child Is Waiting.

Daniel Fienberg, Alan Sepinwall, and Linda Holmes ranked 11 sports films of the 1980s with Personal Best at #1. Rocky III was vetoed by Fienberg; The Natural, Bull Durham, and Raging Bull also made the list. Fienberg won.

Gina Ippolito and Jordan Morris ranked the best action films starring older actors ("AARP-eligible") with Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One at #1. The Limey and The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent were vetoed; Morris won 3-0.

Clay Keller, Ryan Marker, and Thomas Grabinski ranked 13 Juliette Binoche films with Three Colours: Blue at #1. The Unbearable Lightness of Being had its veto overridden; multiple vetoes across rounds made this one of the most contentious mini-Megas.

A three-part Super Draft across January 2024 with Kenny Neibart, Phil Iscove, Emily St. James, Joe Reid, Chris Feil, Katey Rich, Bryan Cogman, Oriana Nudo, and Roxana Hadadi ranking Scorsese's full filmography. The Age of Innocence made the largest veto-recovery jump in show history — from position 29 to #1; the first Patreon Members veto override also occurred.

Ryan Marker and Marya Gates ranked seven Criterion-released world cinema films not previously drafted on the show with Sambizanga at #1. The episode highlighted overlooked international films from Limite to The Color of Pomegranates.
Drea Clark, Ify Nwadiwe, and Inkoo Kang ranked 11 Scorsese-produced films with Uncut Gems at #1. Multiple vetoes and overrides shaped a list that included The Grifters and The Eternal Daughter.
Kyle Turner and Juan Barquin ranked the best queer comedy films live with Some Like It Hot at #1. No rollover vetoes were used; the Mooch Rule was suspended.

Elric Kane and Blake Howard ranked the best Australian exploitation films with Roadgames at #1. Wake in Fright was removed via Commissioner Override; Mad Max, The Proposition, and Razorback all made the list.

Scott Wampler, Eric Vespe, Louis Peitzman, and Wynter Mitchell ranked 13 Stephen King adaptations — a sequel to the show's earlier King episode — with The Shawshank Redemption at #1. Multiple vetoes and one override; Doctor Sleep and Gerald's Game earned inclusion alongside classics.
Billy Ray Brewton, Drea Clark, Clay Keller, and Katie Walsh ranked 20 films of 2023 with All of Us Strangers at #1. Asteroid City, Jules, and BlackBerry were all vetoed; Katie Walsh finished first.

Rebekah McKendry and Graham Skipper ranked the best horror musicals with Little Shop of Horrors at #1. The Mooch Rule was suspended; multiple Commissioner Overrides removed selections, making this an unusually rule-heavy episode.
Brian Collins and Penny Cox ranked the best horror films of 2009 with Orphan at #1. Saw VI was vetoed by Cox; Jennifer's Body, Drag Me to Hell, and Thirst all made the list.
Jacqueline Coley and Erik Anderson ranked the best Jodie Foster films with The Silence of the Lambs at #1. Erik Anderson won 3-1; the list ranged from Taxi Driver to Panic Room.

Lucé Tomlin-Brenner and Ryan Marker ranked Shelton's complete filmography with Your Sister's Sister at #1. Humpday and Your Sister's Sister were each vetoed; We Go Way Back was vetoed by Marker; Ryan won 2-1.

Mike Makowsky, Brian Duffield, and Liz Hannah ranked 10 Aaron Sorkin-written films with The Social Network at #1. Steve Jobs, The American President, and The Social Network were all vetoed; Makowsky won.
Chris Hewitt and Nick de Semlyen ranked the best action sequels with Terminator 2: Judgment Day at #1. John Wick: Chapter 2 and Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom were vetoed; Hewitt won 3-1.
William Bibbiani and Walter Chaw ranked the best films about the afterlife with After Life at #1. Heaven Can Wait and After Life were both vetoed; Walter Chaw swept 3-0.
Clarke Wolfe and Fayna Sanchez ranked the best horror anthology films with Trick 'r Treat at #1. Creepshow was vetoed via the "Midsommar Maneuver"; the Mooch Rule was suspended.

Justin LaLiberty and Samm Deighan ranked the best Vinegar Syndrome releases with Corruption at #1. Drew McWeeny served as guest co-commissioner; LaLiberty won 3-1 in a deep dive into cult and exploitation cinema.

Jordan Crucchiola and Darren Franich ranked the best films that were booed at the Cannes Film Festival with Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me at #1. Dancer in the Dark was vetoed — notably Jordan's first veto in 10 appearances; Franich won 3-0.
Dana Schwartz and Phil Iscove ranked the best Natalie Portman films with Black Swan at #1. V for Vendetta was vetoed; Dana Schwartz won 3-2 with Jackie and May December in the top five.

Marc Bernardin, Darren Franich, and Dave Schilling ranked all 10 Planet of the Apes films with Planet of the Apes (1968) at #1. Multiple vetoes including two on Dawn of the Apes; both commissioners received goose eggs on their predictions — a rare shut-out.

Kris Tapley and Ash Crossan ranked the best films that won the MTV Movie Award for Best Kiss with Moonlight at #1. Jackass Forever was vetoed; Moonlight became the first film to appear as the #1 selection across three separate Screen Drafts episodes.
Billy Ray Brewton and Drea Clark ranked the best Geena Davis films with The Long Kiss Goodnight at #1. Quick Change was vetoed; Thelma & Louise, A League of Their Own, and The Fly all made the list.

Joe Reid and Louis Peitzman ranked the Schumacher filmography in a 9-film mini-Mega with The Client at #1. The list featured Falling Down, The Lost Boys, and Batman Forever — rehabilitating Schumacher's legacy.

Libby Hill, Jordan Crucchiola, and Emily St. James ranked 11 films about lesbian relationships and experience with Portrait of a Lady on Fire at #1. The list ranged from Desert Hearts and Carol to Bound and Show Me Love.

Angelique Jackson and Adam B. Vary ranked the best Angela Bassett films with How Stella Got Her Groove Back at #1. How Stella was vetoed by Vary before Jackson claimed it; What's Love Got to Do with It and Malcolm X also made the list.

Bryan Cogman, Oriana Nudo, and Maureen Lee Lenker ranked 13 films of 1939 — Hollywood's greatest year — with The Wizard of Oz at #1. Young Mr. Lincoln was vetoed; Gone with the Wind's veto was overridden by Cogman.

Elliott Kalan and Jordan Morris ranked the best films about children with supernatural abilities with Kiki's Delivery Service at #1. The Tin Drum and Village of the Damned earned high placements; Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse made the top three.
Jon Gabrus and Ryan Stanger ranked the best JCVD films with Bloodsport at #1. No vetoes occurred; Street Fighter earned a high placement, and Lionheart made a surprise top-three finish.

Mia Lee Vicino and Bethy Squires ranked the best films set in retail and commercial spaces with Clerks at #1. The list included The Watermelon Woman, Little Shop of Horrors, and Notting Hill — a surprisingly diverse category.

Drew McWeeny, Drea Clark, and Billy Ray Brewton ranked 13 films of 1994 with Hoop Dreams at #1. Crumb and Chungking Express were removed via Commissioner Override for their US release dates; Forrest Gump was vetoed by Clark.
Roxana Hadadi, Bilge Ebiri, and Brandon Streussnig ranked 13 Michael Mann films with The Jericho Mile at #1. Ferrari and Manhunter were both vetoed; Heat, Thief, and Collateral all made the list.

Emily St. James and Noel Murray ranked the best films about being stranded or marooned with The Black Stallion at #1. I Saw the TV Glow was removed via Commissioner Override; The Red Turtle and Lord of the Flies (1963) earned high placements.

Clay Keller, Billy Ray Brewton, and Darrin Navarro ranked 22 Michael Crichton adaptations with Physical Evidence at #1. Coma was vetoed multiple times; a veto override by Keller saved Congo — a rare override for a film generally considered middling.

Bryan Cogman and Milla Bell-Hart ranked the best Elmore Leonard adaptations with 52 Pick-Up at #1. Life of Crime and Mr. Majestyk were vetoed; Jackie Brown, Out of Sight, and Get Shorty anchored the top of the list.

Alan Sepinwall, Linda Holmes, Jen Yamato, and Daniel Fienberg ranked 13 sports films of the 1990s with Tin Cup at #1. The Sandlot's veto was overridden by Yamato; Jerry Maguire's veto was overridden by Fienberg — two overrides in one episode.

Katie Walsh and Louis Peitzman ranked the best Patricia Highsmith adaptations with Deep Water at #1. The Two Faces of January and The Talented Mr. Ripley were both vetoed; Purple Noon and The American Friend earned strong placements.

Two teams — Joanna Robinson & Katey Rich vs. Chris Feil & Joe Reid — ranked the best films set in bookstores or libraries with Party Girl at #1. The team format created spirited back-and-forth; Desk Set and 84 Charing Cross Road anchored the list.

Kyle Anderson and Billy Ray Brewton ranked the best horror films of 1977 on the show's 300th episode with The Hills Have Eyes at #1. The Car was vetoed; Suspiria, Eraserhead, Rabid, and House all made the list in a banner-year celebration.
Justin LaLiberty and Samm Deighan ranked the best erotic horror films with Trouble Every Day at #1. The Hunger, Crash (1996), and Ganja & Hess all made the list in a genre deep-dive.

Joe George and Josh Larsen ranked the best rural and backwoods horror films with The Dead Don't Die at #1. The Texas Chain Saw Massacre and Lamb were both vetoed; Near Dark and Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me earned inclusion.

Bryan Cogman, B.J. Colangelo, and Chris Hewitt ranked 13 classic Hammer Horror films with The Devil Rides Out at #1. The episode honored the absent Lucé Tomlin-Brenner's list; Frankenstein Must Be Destroyed and Quatermass and the Pit rounded out the top.

William Bibbiani, Walter Chaw, Drea Clark, Adam B. Vary, Mark Harris, Darren Franich, Oriana Nudo, Maureen Lee Lenker, and Ben Mankiewicz ranked all 30 Hitchcock feature films across three parts with Psycho at #1. Drea Clark earned "Screen Drafts Legend" status during this epic multi-part draft.

Clay Keller, Ryan Marker, and Marya Gates ranked 21 Truffaut films across two parts with The 400 Blows at #1. A Patreon member protection was used; Jules and Jim was selected by Patreon vote in Part II.

Clarke Wolfe and Samm Levine ranked the best holiday horror films not set at Christmas or Halloween with Jaws at #1. Midsommar and My Bloody Valentine 3D both made the list; Jaws as a July 4th horror film was the episode's central argument.

Liz Shannon Miller and Angie Han ranked all 9 Charlie Kaufman-scripted and directed films with Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind at #1. Synecdoche, New York was vetoed multiple times; Being John Malkovich earned a top-three finish.
Clay Keller and Ryan Marker ranked all 5 Whit Stillman films in a Kristmas Eve mini-Super Draft with The Last Days of Disco at #1. Barcelona was vetoed once; Bryan Cogman served as commissioner.

Ryan Marker, Phil Iscove, Kenny Neibart, and Carrie Wittmer ranked 13 films of 1999 with American Movie at #1. This was billed as the first "redraft" in Screen Drafts history, revisiting the year previously covered in episode 37; Ghost Dog's veto was overridden.

Dane McDonald and Oriana Nudo ranked all 8 Sofia Coppola features with Marie Antoinette at #1. Lost in Translation was vetoed by Patreon members — a memorable crowd-driven veto; The Virgin Suicides and Somewhere also made the top five.

Joanna Robinson, Bryan Cogman, Drew McWeeny, Roxana Hadadi, Blake Howard, and Ryan Marker ranked all 24 Coppola features across two parts with The Godfather Part II at #1. Megalopolis earned an early placement; multiple vetoes shaped the bottom half of the list.

Billy Ray Brewton and Ryan Marker ranked the best films produced by American Zoetrope (Coppola's production company) with American Graffiti at #1. Koyaanisqatsi and Hearts of Darkness also made the list.

Rebekah McKendry and Elric Kane ranked the best internet-themed horror films with Pulse at #1. My Little Eye was vetoed; Host, Red Rooms, and We're All Going to the World's Fair represented the modern age of digital horror.

Katey Rich and Chris Feil ranked the Minnelli filmography with Meet Me in St. Louis at #1. Bells Are Ringing and The Clock were vetoed; Some Came Running and The Bad and the Beautiful earned high placements.

Ryan Marker, Billy Ray Brewton, Alonso Duralde, and Drea Clark ranked 20 films of 2024 with No Other Land at #1. Anora was vetoed twice; Challengers' veto was overridden by Marker; Drea Clark came first.

Drea Clark vs. Patreon Members (via Drew McWeeny) ranked the best Parker Posey films with Party Girl at #1. No vetoes occurred; Waiting for Guffman, Best in Show, and Dazed and Confused all made the list.

Louis Peitzman and Alison Willmore ranked the best vacation horror films with The Vanishing (1988) at #1. No vetoes occurred; The Descent, An American Werewolf in London, and Infinity Pool all made the list.

Graham Skipper and Jill Gevargizian ranked all 9 Texas Chainsaw Massacre films with The Texas Chain Saw Massacre at #1. Texas Chainsaw 3D was vetoed twice by Skipper; TCM: The Beginning was also vetoed.
Dane McDonald and Katie Walsh ranked the best Timothée Chalamet films with Call Me by Your Name at #1. Lady Bird was vetoed by McDonald; Dune: Part Two and Bones and All also made the top five.
Billy Ray Brewton and Ben Hethcoat ranked the best true crime documentaries with The Thin Blue Line at #1. No vetoes occurred; Dear Zachary, The Act of Killing, and Paradise Lost 3 all made the list.

Chancellor Agard, Ryan Marker, and Kyle Anderson ranked 11 DC/CW television shows with Superman & Lois at #1. This was the first Screen Drafts episode to draft exclusively television programs; Doom Patrol and Supergirl were each vetoed.

Darren Franich and Adam B. Vary ranked the best films written or directed by the Gilroy brothers with Nightcrawler at #1. No vetoes or overrides occurred; Michael Clayton, The Bourne Identity, and The Cutting Edge all made the list.
B.J. Colangelo and Harmony Colangelo ranked the best body swap films with Your Name at #1. Your Name was vetoed by Harmony before being rescued; Face/Off and Freaky Friday (2003) also made the list.

Kevin Tully and Mike Scollins ranked films adapted from Disney theme park attractions with Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl at #1. Tomorrowland and Dead Man's Chest were vetoed; Mission to Mars and Country Bears earned affectionate inclusions.

Phil Iscove and Esther Zuckerman ranked the best high school romantic comedies of the 1990s with Clueless at #1. Rushmore was vetoed by Zuckerman; Can't Hardly Wait and 10 Things I Hate About You also made the list.

Bryan Cogman, Mitchell Beaupre, Milla Bell-Hart, and Roxana Hadadi (via Ryan Marker) ranked 13 Gene Hackman films with The Conversation at #1. The Royal Tenenbaums and Hoosiers each had vetoes overridden; Unforgiven and The French Connection also made the list.

Eric Vespe and Mallory O'Meara ranked 10 Kathryn Bigelow films with Strange Days at #1. The list ranged from The Loveless to Zero Dark Thirty; Near Dark, The Hurt Locker, and Point Break all made the top five.

In a special seven-legend draft, Darren Franich, Drea Clark, Clay Keller, Bryan Cogman, Billy Ray Brewton, Ryan Marker, and Graham Skipper each ranked one Mission: Impossible film using "rabbit's foot" mechanics with a single veto and override per person. Mission: Impossible – Fallout earned two veto attempts — the most vetoed film in the draft.

Billy Ray Brewton and Katie Walsh ranked the best films starring Corey Haim and/or Corey Feldman with The Lost Boys at #1. Dream a Little Dream was vetoed; Stand by Me and The 'Burbs also made the list.

William Bibbiani and Clem Bastow ranked the best time-loop films with Edge of Tomorrow at #1. The list featured Triangle, The Endless, and Source Code — favoring darker and more structurally complex takes on the premise.

Guy Branum and Wynter Mitchell ranked the best Cameron Crowe films with Jerry Maguire at #1. Vanilla Sky was vetoed by Mitchell and Singles was vetoed by Branum — each party blocking one of the other's picks.

Patrick Willems and Matthew Krol ranked the best action comedies with Grosse Pointe Blank at #1. Demolition Man was vetoed; Hot Fuzz, Midnight Run, Big Trouble in Little China, and Kung Fu Hustle all made the list.

Drew McWeeny and Jordan Crucchiola ranked 9 Shanley-written films with Joe Versus the Volcano at #1. Moonstruck, Congo, and Joe Versus the Volcano all entered the Marquee of Fame; Doubt achieved a "Hat Trick" — its third Screen Drafts appearance.

Thomas Vitale (former Syfy president) and Alex Terapane ranked the best Syfy original movies with Sharknado 2: The Second One at #1. The list celebrated Syfy's creature-feature legacy from Sharktopus to Ghost Shark.
Demma Strausbaugh, Cyntisha Coats, and Imani Davis ranked 11 Neon-distributed films with Parasite at #1. Pig and Ingrid Goes West were vetoed; Anatomy of a Fall and Anora also made the list.

Bill Bria and Ashley Coffin ranked the best standalone (non-franchise) science fiction films with Interstellar at #1. Total Recall was vetoed; E.T., District 9, and The Fifth Element all made the list.

Josh Miller and Matt Mercer ranked the Peter Jackson filmography with The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring at #1. The Frighteners and King Kong were each vetoed; Bad Taste, Braindead, and Heavenly Creatures all made the list.

Daniel Fienberg, Linda Holmes, Jen Yamato, and Alan Sepinwall ranked 13 sports films of the 2000s with Love & Basketball at #1. Love & Basketball was vetoed twice by Sepinwall; Lagaan, Bend it Like Beckham, and The Wrestler all made the list.
Ryan Marker and Kenny Neibart ranked the best car racing films with Grand Prix at #1. Le Mans was vetoed twice and Grand Prix was also vetoed; Ford v. Ferrari and Speed Racer also made the list.

Two teams — "Team This Had Oscar Buzz" vs. "Team Chasing Amy Adams" — ranked the best films in which an actress won the Best Supporting Actress Oscar in the 21st century with Chicago at #1. Dreamgirls was vetoed; the team format created an unusual collaborative drafting experience.

Clay Keller and Dave Schilling ranked their favorite films from summer 2025 with Sinners at #1. No predictions were made due to the last-minute nature of the episode; Superman, 28 Years Later, and Final Destination Bloodlines all made the list.

Katie Walsh and Milla Bell-Hart ranked the best films set at or about women in college with Legally Blonde at #1. Legally Blonde was self-vetoed by Walsh before she rescued it; Spring Breakers and Black Christmas each achieved "Grand Slam" status.

Ryan Marker and Steve Berg ranked the best cryptid-focused films with Demonwarp at #1. Incident at Loch Ness and Sasquatch Sunset were vetoed; Harry and the Hendersons earned an affectionate top placement.

Billy Ray Brewton and Graham Skipper ranked the best mockumentary films with Lake Mungo at #1. Apollo 11 was removed via Commissioner Override; Popstar: Never Stop Never Stopping was self-vetoed; Best in Show achieved a Hat Trick.
Bryan Cogman, Oriana Nudo, and Drew McWeeny ranked 13 comedy films of the 1970s with Blazing Saddles at #1. Real Life, Blazing Saddles, and Life of Brian were vetoed; four films achieved Hat Tricks.

Billy Ray Brewton, Clark Collis, and Kyle Anderson ranked 13 horror films of the 2000s with The Ring at #1. The House of the Devil and Signs were vetoed; Shaun of the Dead and 28 Days Later achieved Hat Tricks.

DeVaughn Taylor and Mary Beth McAndrews ranked all 7 Paranormal Activity franchise films with Paranormal Activity at #1. Paranormal Activity: The Marked Ones and Paranormal Activity were both vetoed; Paranormal Activity 3 achieved a Hat Trick and Paranormal Activity itself achieved a Grand Slam.

Eric Plese and Ryan Estrada ranked the best Korean horror films with A Tale of Two Sisters at #1. The Chaser and Train to Busan (self-veto) were vetoed; The Wailing and The Housemaid also made the list; The Housemaid entered the Marquee of Fame.

B.J. Colangelo, Teri Gamble, and Julia Marchese ranked 13 female-directed horror films with Pet Sematary (Mary Lambert) at #1. Hellbender and Ghostwatch were vetoed; Jennifer's Body, The Substance, and Near Dark all made the list.

Dave Schilling and Danielle Radford ranked the best Dario Argento films with Deep Red at #1. Suspiria, Deep Red, and Opera were each vetoed in various rounds; Tenebre and Deep Red entered the Marquee of Fame; Suspiria achieved a Hat Trick.

Bryan Cogman and Maureen Lee Lenker ranked 8 Flynn-de Havilland collaborations with Captain Blood at #1. Four's a Crowd was vetoed; The Adventures of Robin Hood entered the Marquee of Fame and became a Unified #1; Lenker achieved MVP status.
William Bibbiani and Darren Mooney ranked the best films about chess with Searching for Bobby Fischer at #1. Fresh entered the Marquee of Fame; Searching for Bobby Fischer achieved a Hat Trick.

Darren Franich, Jordan Crucchiola, and Louis Peitzman ranked 17 Verhoeven features with Showgirls at #1. RoboCop, Basic Instinct, and Showgirls each achieved Hat Trick status; Total Recall achieved a Grand Slam — the most decorated films in a single episode.

Bryan Cogman, Joanna Robinson, Graham Skipper, and Maureen Lee Lenker ranked 13 films about theatre with All That Jazz at #1. The list featured Hamnet, Topsy-Turvy, Opening Night, and Tick Tick Boom — a rich survey of theatrical-world cinema.

Ryan Marker, Mitchell Beaupre, and Walter Chaw ranked all 35 Robert Altman features across two episodes (Part I in November 2025, Part II in April 2026) with M*A*S*H among the most contested selections. Multiple vetoes across all four parts shaped the rankings; M*A*S*H was vetoed before landing mid-list.

Riley Stearns and Thomas Grabinski ranked the full Lanthimos filmography with The Killing of a Sacred Deer at #1. Bugonia was vetoed before being reselected; Grabinski won 3-0; My Best Friend was excluded due to Lanthimos' disavowal of the film.
Josh Miller and Henry McComas ranked the best snow-set horror films with The Thing at #1. The Last Winter was vetoed; Let the Right One In, Dead Snow, and 30 Days of Night all made the list.

Imani Davis, Maureen Lee Lenker, and Oriana Nudo ranked 11 Sidney Poitier films with In the Heat of the Night at #1. A guest veto was overridden on The Defiant Ones; Sneakers was vetoed; Lenker came first.

Clay Keller and Ryan Marker ranked the best Miriam Hopkins films on Christmas Eve with The Story of Temple Drake at #1. Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde was vetoed before returning; the episode ended in a 3-3 tie decided by coin flip — the first tie in Screen Drafts history.
Clay Keller, Darren Franich, Roxana Hadadi, and Emily St. James ranked 13 films of 2000 with Yi Yi at #1. Wonder Boys was vetoed twice; Ghost Dog and Yi Yi were also vetoed; Clay came first.

Jordan Crucchiola, Dave Schilling, and William Bibbiani ranked 15 Paul W.S. Anderson films as the first installment in January 2026's "Anderson Month" with Shopping at #1. Event Horizon was vetoed before being rescued; Mortal Kombat was awarded to Patreon members. William Bibbiani won.

Clay Keller, Bryan Cogman, Billy Ray Brewton, and Drea Clark ranked 13 Wes Anderson films in the second week of Anderson Month with The Royal Tenenbaums at #1. Drea's veto of Moonrise Kingdom was overridden by Billy Ray; Patreon Members vetoed The Royal Tenenbaums before Keller reclaimed it. Drea Clark won.

Oriana Nudo, Darren Franich, and Graham Skipper ranked the Brad Anderson filmography with Session 9 at #1 in the third Anderson Month installment. Multiple veto overrides occurred; Nudo was inaugurated as a Screen Drafts Legend during this episode.

Ryan Marker, Katie Walsh, and Cory Everett ranked the Paul Thomas Anderson filmography with There Will Be Blood at #1. Magnolia's veto was overridden by Walsh in the episode's defining moment; Katie Walsh won.

Justin LaLiberty and Brandon Streussnig ranked the best action horror films with Cobra at #1. The Heroic Trio was vetoed by LaLiberty; Near Dark, Resident Evil: Retribution, and Maniac Cop 2 all made the list.
Marya Gates and Robert Daniels ranked the best films featuring the Ethan Hawke & Richard Linklater collaboration with Before Midnight at #1.

Clay Keller and Blake Howard ranked all 9 BMW Films "The Hire" short films directed by top filmmakers. Blake Howard vetoed multiple Clay Keller selections — Beat the Devil twice and Hostage — in a spirited competition over this unique branded-content series.

Drew McWeeny and Bobby Roberts ranked 8 films of 1980 with The Shining at #1. Airplane! was vetoed by Roberts; The Empire Strikes Back was vetoed by McWeeny — both major films falling to vetoes in a banner-year draft.
Ryan Marker, Drea Clark, Katie Walsh, and Daniel Waters ranked 20 films of 2025 with One Battle After Another at #1. Daniel Waters overrode Katie Walsh's veto of Blue Moon; Katie Walsh came first.

Clay Keller and Phil Iscove ranked the best Holly Hunter films with Broadcast News at #1. Clay Keller self-vetoed Incredibles 2; Home for the Holidays was vetoed by Iscove; Crash and Living Out Loud also made the list.

Daniel Kraus and Greg Nicotero ranked the best George A. Romero films with Night of the Living Dead at #1. Knightriders was vetoed by Nicotero; Dawn of the Dead, Martin, and Day of the Dead all made the list.
Rosalie Kicks and Ryan Silberstein ranked the best films about nuns with Black Narcissus at #1. Doubt was vetoed by Silberstein; Viridiana was vetoed by Kicks; the list ranged from The Devils to Ida.

Bryan Cogman and Oriana Nudo ranked the best Diane Keaton films with Reds at #1. Oriana self-vetoed Manhattan Murder Mystery after learning its release date followed sexual misconduct allegations — a rare ethics-based veto; Annie Hall and The Godfather Part II also made the list.

Clark Collis, Piya Sinha-Roy, and Billy Ray Brewton ranked 11 21st-century British indie films with In the Loop at #1. Starred Up, Eden Lake, and Under the Skin were all vetoed; Four Lions and Attack the Block rounded out an eclectic list.
